GCE A LEVEL MARKING SCHEME

 SUMMER 2024 (Predicted)

 A LEVEL
 ECONOMICS – COMPONENT 3
 A520U30-1A – Advanced

 _________________________________________________________________________

 About this marking scheme
 The purpose of this marking scheme is to provide teachers, learners, and other interested parties,
 with an understanding of the assessment criteria used to assess this specific assessment.

 This marking scheme reflects the criteria by which this assessment was marked in a live series and
 was finalised following detailed discussion at an examiners' conference. A team of qualified
 examiners were trained specifically in the application of this marking scheme. The aim of the
 conference was to ensure that the marking scheme was interpreted and applied in the same way
 by all examiners. It may not be possible, or appropriate, to capture every variation that a candidate
 may present in their responses within this marking scheme. However, during the training
 conference, examiners were guided in using their professional judgement to credit alternative valid
 responses as instructed by the document, and through reviewing exemplar responses.

 Without the benefit of participation in the examiners' conference, teachers, learners and other
 users, may have different views on certain matters of detail or interpretation. Therefore, it is
 strongly recommended that this marking scheme is used alongside other guidance, such as
 published exemplar materials or Guidance for Teaching. This marking scheme is final and will not
 be changed, unless in the event that a clear error is identified, as it reflects the criteria used to
 assess candidate responses during the live series.
 _________________________________________________________________________


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                         1
 GENERAL MARKING GUIDANCE

 Positive Marking
 It should be remembered that learners are writing under examination conditions and credit should
 be given for what the learner writes, rather than adopting the approach of penalising him/her for
 any omissions. It should be possible for a very good response to achieve full marks and a very
 poor one to achieve zero marks. Marks should not be deducted for a less than perfect answer if it
 satisfies the criteria of the mark scheme, nor should marks be added as a consolation where they
 are not merited.

 For each question there is a list of indicative content which suggest the range of economic
 concepts, theory, issues and arguments which might be included in learners' answers. This is not
 intended to be exhaustive and learners do not have to include all the indicative content to reach the
 highest level of the mark scheme.

 The level-based mark schemes sub-divide the total mark to allocate to individual assessment
 objectives. These are shown in bands in the mark scheme. For each assessment objective a
 descriptor will indicate the different skills and qualities at the appropriate level. Learner's responses
 to questions are assessed against the relevant individual assessment objectives and they may
 achieve different bands within a single question. A mark will be awarded for each assessment
 objective targeted in the question and then totalled to give an overall mark for the question.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                             2
 Section A.
 1. Tech giants accused of using "dark patterns" to manipulate consumer choices
 1 1 Explain what is meant by information asymmetry and how it can lead to market failure. [10]

                                     AO1                                             AO3
Band                                6 marks                                        4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                          3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of information asymmetry       Strong chains of reasoning linking asymmetric
           including Akerlof's "market for lemons", adverse   information to misallocation of resources, market
           selection and moral hazard.                        breakdown, and welfare loss.
2          3-4 marks                                          1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                     Limited analysis
           Concept understood but key elements (adverse       Weak chains.
           selection/moral hazard) lacking detail.
1          1-2 marks                                          1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                  Limited analysis
           Basic definition only.                             Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                            0 marks
           No relevant knowledge.                             No valid analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 Information asymmetry exists when one party in an economic transaction has more or better
 information than the other. This violates the neoclassical assumption of perfect information.
 Adverse selection: occurs before the transaction – informed parties self-select, leading to a
 skewed pool of participants (Akerlof's "lemons" in second-hand cars; healthy people opting out of
 insurance).
 Moral hazard: occurs after the transaction – the insured party changes behaviour knowing the
 consequences fall on someone else (risky behaviour after buying insurance; banks taking excess
 risk if bailed out).
 Principal-agent problem: agents (managers, employees) act in their own interest rather than the
 principal's (shareholders).
 Examples in digital markets: dark patterns, hidden terms, opaque algorithms, consent fatigue.

 AO3
 Asymmetric information leads to allocative inefficiency: consumers under-consume goods they
 suspect are "lemons"; over-consume goods whose risks they don't see.
 In the extreme, markets can collapse entirely (no buyers willing to pay even for high-quality goods).
 In credit markets, adverse selection can lead to credit rationing and inefficient under-lending
 (Stiglitz-Weiss).
 In healthcare and insurance markets, government intervention (NHS, mandates) is often justified to
 address these failures.
 Dark patterns in digital markets exploit consumer information deficits, generating hidden costs and
 reducing welfare.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                  3
 1 2 Discuss the extent to which behavioural economics has challenged the assumptions of
 traditional neoclassical economics. [20]

                       AO1                                  AO3                                     AO4
Band                 6 marks                             6 marks                               8 marks
3        5-6 marks                           5-6 marks                             6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                 Excellent analysis                    Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of              Strong reasoning on how specific      Two-sided answer assessing the
         behavioural economics concepts      findings challenge rational utility   robustness and limits of
         (bounded rationality, heuristics,   maximisation.                         behavioural insights. Reasoned
         framing, loss aversion, nudges)                                           judgment.
         and their origin.
2        3-4 marks                           3-4 marks                             3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                      Good analysis                         Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                     Good argument.                        Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                           1-2 marks                             1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                   Limited analysis                      Limited evaluation
         Few points.                         Superficial.                          Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                             0 marks                               0 marks
         No knowledge.                       No analysis.                          No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Behavioural challenges)
 Bounded rationality (Simon): individuals lack the cognitive capacity, time and information to
 make fully rational decisions, instead satisficing.
 Heuristics (Kahneman & Tversky): rules of thumb (availability, representativeness, anchoring)
 cause systematic biases.
 Loss aversion and prospect theory: losses hurt roughly twice as much as equivalent gains,
 contradicting expected utility theory.
 Framing effects: the way choices are presented affects decisions, violating rational choice
 consistency.
 Present bias / hyperbolic discounting: people over-value present consumption, under-save for
 the future (retirement, diet, exercise).
 Herd behaviour and social norms: people copy others even when inconsistent with self-interest.
 Nudge theory (Thaler): choice architecture can steer decisions – default options, social proof,
 simplification. Implemented by the UK Behavioural Insights Team.
 These findings challenge the core neoclassical assumptions of rationality, self-interest and fixed
 preferences.

 AO1/AO4 (Limits of the challenge)
 Rational choice remains a powerful approximation – most big economic decisions (houses, jobs,
 investments) are made with considerable deliberation.
 Markets provide learning and discipline – irrational behaviour is costly and gets corrected over
 repeated decisions.
 Arbitrage can eliminate mispricing in aggregate, even if individual traders are biased.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                    4
 Experimental findings may not generalise beyond the lab to real market contexts.
 Behavioural economics has been criticised for lacking a unified theory – it is a collection of
 anomalies rather than a systematic alternative.
 The "nudge" agenda has had mixed real-world success – some interventions fail to replicate
 (auto-enrolment strong, many others weaker).
 Many findings are incorporated into mainstream economics (information economics, game theory,
 contract theory) without abandoning rational choice.
 Behavioural economics complements rather than replaces neoclassical theory – Richard Thaler
 himself emphasised they work together.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the domain – behavioural effects strong in savings, health, gambling; weaker in
 high-stakes repeated decisions.
 Depends on how we define "rationality" – narrow vs broad.
 Behavioural insights have genuinely improved public policy design (pension auto-enrolment, organ
 donation opt-out).
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                    5
 2. Premier League clubs investigated by CMA over potential anti-competitive agreements
 2 1 Outline what is meant by game theory and explain its application to oligopolistic markets. [10]

                                  AO1                                              AO3
Band                           6 marks                                           4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                         3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                               Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of game theory, payoff        Strong chains of reasoning showing how strategic
           matrices, dominant strategies and Nash            interdependence generates stable, often
           equilibrium. Prisoner's dilemma used well.        suboptimal outcomes.
2          3-4 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Concepts partially grasped; Nash equilibrium or   Weak chains.
           payoff matrices may be weak.
1          1-2 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Basic understanding only.                         Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                           0 marks
           No knowledge.                                     No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 Game theory is the mathematical study of strategic decision-making where outcomes depend on
 the actions of multiple interdependent agents (von Neumann, Morgenstern; later Nash).
 Key concepts: players, strategies, payoffs, dominant strategy, Nash equilibrium (a stable outcome
 where no player can improve by unilaterally deviating).
 Oligopoly markets exhibit mutual interdependence – each firm's profit depends on its rivals'
 decisions (price, output, advertising, R&D;).
 A payoff matrix can be used to illustrate the classic prisoner's dilemma applied to pricing decisions.

 AO3 (application to oligopoly)
 Prisoner's dilemma pricing: two firms both better off by colluding (high price), but each has an
 incentive to defect (cut price). Result: low-price Nash equilibrium despite both preferring the
 collusive outcome.
 Kinked demand curve (Sweezy): rivals match price cuts but not price rises, producing price
 rigidity – a form of implicit game-theoretic equilibrium.
 Tit-for-tat and repeated games: cooperation (collusion) becomes sustainable in repeated games
 through reputation and retaliation.
 Cartels, price leadership and tacit collusion are ways oligopolists try to escape the low-price Nash
 equilibrium.
 Football markets: broadcast rights negotiations, transfer windows and salary co-ordination are
 classic game-theoretic contexts where CMA investigations arise.
 Entry deterrence and limit pricing can be modelled as sequential games (Stackelberg).
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                6
 2 2 Evaluate whether price discrimination is always against the consumer interest. [20]

                       AO1                                 AO3                               AO4
Band                 6 marks                            6 marks                         8 marks
3        5-6 marks                          5-6 marks                       6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge                Excellent analysis              Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of the three   Strong reasoning on consumer    Well-developed two-sided answer
         degrees of price discrimination,   and producer surplus effects.   with reasoned judgment.
         conditions required, and
         examples.
2        3-4 marks                          3-4 marks                       3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                     Good analysis                   Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                    Good argument.                  Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                          1-2 marks                       1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                  Limited analysis                Limited evaluation
         Few points.                        Superficial.                    Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                            0 marks                         0 marks
         No knowledge.                      No analysis.                    No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Against consumer interest)
 First-degree PD extracts all consumer surplus, transferring it entirely to the producer. Increasingly
 feasible through algorithmic personalised pricing.
 Third-degree PD (by market segment: student, senior, peak/off-peak) charges inelastic segments
 more – consumers with inelastic demand (often poorest, most desperate, least informed) pay
 higher prices.
 Reduces overall consumer surplus, increasing producer surplus – a transfer from consumers to
 firms.
 Requires market power – price discrimination signals anticompetitive conditions.
 Can be used to deter new entrants and sustain market dominance.
 "Drip pricing" and hidden charges (common in streaming, airlines, financial services) exploit
 bounded rationality.
 Raises fairness concerns – especially when algorithmic pricing uses data on willingness to pay,
 income, or location.

 AO1/AO4 (Can benefit consumers)
 Price discrimination can expand output beyond what a single-price monopoly would produce,
 reducing deadweight loss.
 Some consumers who could not afford the single monopoly price can now access the good (e.g.
 off-peak rail fares, student discounts, pensioner rates) – increases access.
 Second-degree PD (bulk discounts, loyalty programmes) can pass economies of scale on to large
 customers.
 Cross-subsidisation: high-paying segments subsidise low-paying ones, maintaining access for
 socially desirable groups.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                              7
 In natural monopolies with high fixed costs, price discrimination can be the only way to cover total
 costs and keep the service running (rail, utilities, pharmaceuticals).
 Can fund innovation: high-income markets pay premium prices which cover R&D; that eventually
 benefits all (generic drug markets).

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the degree (first-degree clearly worst, third-degree more mixed).
 Depends on the market – life-saving drugs vs airline tickets have very different welfare
 implications.
 Depends on whether PD expands output or simply redistributes surplus.
 Depends on regulatory framework and whether firms have other forms of market power.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                        8
 Section B.
 3. Bank of England independence under political scrutiny after rate hike controversy
 3 1 Using a diagram, explain the transmission mechanism of monetary policy. [10]

                                   AO1                                          AO3
Band                              6 marks                                     4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                      3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                            Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of the transmission        Strong chains of reasoning through each channel
           mechanism showing multiple channels. Clear,    (borrowing costs, asset prices, exchange rate,
           well-labelled flow diagram.                    expectations).
2          3-4 marks                                      1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Most channels identified but development       Weak chains.
           superficial.
1          1-2 marks                                      1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                              Limited analysis
           Basic understanding.                           Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                        0 marks
           No knowledge.                                  No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 The monetary transmission mechanism describes how changes in monetary policy instruments
 (Bank Rate, asset purchases) affect AD, output and inflation.
 A flow diagram would show Bank Rate → market interest rates → borrowing/saving, asset prices,
 exchange rates, expectations → consumption, investment, net exports → AD → output and
 inflation.
 The Bank of England typically acts via the MPC setting Bank Rate every six weeks.

 AO3 (main channels)
 Interest rate channel: higher Bank Rate raises borrowing costs (mortgages, business loans),
 reducing consumption and investment.
 Asset price channel: higher rates reduce bond and equity prices, lowering household wealth and
 consumer spending (wealth effect).
 Exchange rate channel: higher rates attract capital inflows, strengthening the currency – imports
 cheaper (lower imported inflation), exports dearer (lower X).
 Expectations / credibility channel: credible rate changes anchor inflation expectations, feeding
 into wage/price setting.
 Bank lending channel: higher rates tighten credit conditions, further reducing bank lending to
 firms and households.
 QE/QT channel: asset purchases directly affect long-term interest rates and risk premia when
 Bank Rate is constrained.
 All channels affect AD, ultimately influencing output and the price level.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                            9
 3 2 Evaluate whether central bank independence has been successful in achieving
 macroeconomic objectives. [20]

                      AO1                              AO3                              AO4
Band                6 marks                         6 marks                        8 marks
3        5-6 marks                      5-6 marks                      6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge            Excellent analysis             Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of CB      Strong reasoning linking       Two-sided, reasoned judgment
         independence (UK 1997, ECB,    independence to credibility,   considering recent challenges
         Fed) and macroeconomic         inflation anchoring and        (inflation, QE side-effects,
         outcomes.                      outcomes.                      democratic accountability).
2        3-4 marks                      3-4 marks                      3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                 Good analysis                  Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                Good argument.                 Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                      1-2 marks                      1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge              Limited analysis               Limited evaluation
         Few points.                    Superficial.                   Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                        0 marks                        0 marks
         No knowledge.                  No analysis.                   No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For success)
 Independence removes short-term political incentives to inflate the economy pre-election (political
 business cycles).
 Credible commitment to low inflation anchors inflation expectations, reducing the short-run output
 cost of disinflation (sacrifice ratio).
 Time consistency problem (Kydland-Prescott) solved: independent central banks commit to rules
 rather than falling to discretionary pressures.
 UK inflation averaged close to the 2% target for over a decade post-1997 (until 2021-22 shocks),
 indicating success.
 Independence enabled rapid and large response to 2008 crisis (QE, emergency rate cuts) and
 Covid (further QE, Covid corporate bond facility).
 Reduced risk premia on government bonds – lower long-run borrowing costs for the UK.
 International consensus (IMF, OECD) that independence improves macroeconomic outcomes on
 average.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / limitations)
 Democratic deficit: unelected technocrats make decisions with huge distributional consequences
 (mortgage holders vs savers, rich vs poor).
 QE has had major distributional effects (inflating asset prices, benefiting wealthy), arguably a
 political decision.
 Central banks missed the 2008 crisis and the recent inflation surge – highlighting forecasting limits
 and model failures.
 Post-pandemic inflation (10%+ in the UK) raised questions about whether the Bank acted too
 slowly, damaging credibility.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                       10
 Over-reliance on monetary policy leaves fiscal policy overused in crises.
 Independence can be formal but not real: recent political interference (Trump, Turkey, Truss
 premiership) undermines credibility.
 Coordination problems with fiscal policy – independent CB tightening can fight expansionary fiscal
 policy, raising deadweight losses.
 Single mandate (inflation) ignores other objectives (employment, financial stability, climate),
 leading to sub-optimal trade-offs.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the definition of success (inflation control vs broader objectives).
 Depends on the country context and quality of institutional design.
 Recent experience has revealed limits but not necessarily invalidated the principle.
 Could complement rather than replace political oversight.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                  11
 4. UK economy shows signs of a persistent negative output gap
 4 1 Explain what is meant by an output gap and how it might be identified. [10]

                                  AO1                                               AO3
Band                            6 marks                                           4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                         3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                               Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of positive/negative output   Strong chains on how indicators (unemployment,
           gaps. Accurate AD/AS or Y-time diagram.           capacity utilisation, inflation, wage growth) can
                                                             reveal output gaps.
2          3-4 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Concept understood; indicators superficial.       Weak chains.
1          1-2 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Basic.                                            Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                           0 marks
           No knowledge.                                     No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 The output gap is the difference between actual real GDP and the economy's potential (trend)
 output, expressed as a percentage of potential output.
 Negative output gap: actual < potential, implying spare capacity, unemployment above NAIRU,
 and downward pressure on inflation.
 Positive output gap: actual > potential, implying over-utilisation, labour shortages and upward
 pressure on inflation.
 Potential output depends on supply-side factors: quantity and quality of labour and capital,
 productivity, technology.

 AO3 (identification)
 Direct measurement is difficult because potential output is unobservable. Econometric techniques
 (HP filter, production function) estimate it with large uncertainty.
 Labour market indicators: unemployment rate relative                       to   NAIRU,      wage      growth,
 vacancies-to-unemployed ratio, long-term unemployment.
 Business survey data: capacity utilisation rates (e.g. CBI industrial trends survey), PMIs,
 business investment intentions.
 Inflation signals: core inflation below target for a sustained period suggests a negative gap;
 persistent above-target inflation suggests positive.
 Productivity growth: weak productivity may indicate a negative gap or a structurally lower
 potential.
 Bank of England MPC: produces its own estimates using a suite of indicators.
 OBR Fiscal Sustainability Report: publishes output gap estimates for fiscal forecasting.
 Uncertainty: historical revisions to output gap estimates are frequent and large, complicating
 real-time policy-making (UK has a well-known "productivity puzzle").
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                 12
 4 2 Evaluate the most appropriate policy response to a prolonged negative output gap. [20]

                       AO1                              AO3                                 AO4
Band                 6 marks                         6 marks                            8 marks
3        5-6 marks                       5-6 marks                         6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge             Excellent analysis                Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of policy   Strong chains of reasoning on     Well-developed two-sided answer,
         options (demand-side and        how policies can close the gap.   reasoned judgment on policy mix.
         supply-side).
2        3-4 marks                       3-4 marks                         3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                  Good analysis                     Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                 Good argument.                    Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                       1-2 marks                         1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge               Limited analysis                  Limited evaluation
         Few points.                     Superficial.                      Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                         0 marks                           0 marks
         No knowledge.                   No analysis.                      No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (Demand-side response)
 Expansionary monetary policy: cut Bank Rate, use QE to lower long-term rates. Channels: lower
 borrowing costs, currency depreciation, wealth effects.
 Expansionary fiscal policy: cut taxes or raise government spending. Multiplier effects amplify the
 impact in a negative gap.
 Automatic stabilisers kick in without delay – benefits and progressive tax structure cushion the
 downturn.
 Forward guidance and commitment to lower-for-longer rates support expectations.
 Countercyclical policy avoids hysteresis – prolonged unemployment destroys human capital and
 permanently lowers potential output.

 AO1/AO4 (Supply-side and limitations)
 Supply-side reforms (infrastructure investment, education, planning reform, innovation support)
 raise potential output, shrinking the gap from above.
 Risk of fiscal unsustainability: high UK debt (100%+ of GDP) constrains stimulus, particularly in
 markets sensitive to bond yields.
 Monetary policy may be constrained by the Zero Lower Bound, limiting conventional stimulus.
 The persistent "productivity puzzle" suggests the output gap may be smaller than estimates imply –
 stimulating a constrained economy risks inflation rather than growth.
 Crowding out and Ricardian equivalence can weaken fiscal multipliers.
 Distributional effects of QE (asset price inflation) have raised political concerns.
 Structural issues (skills shortages, regional inequalities, Brexit friction) require supply-side, not
 demand-side, remedies.
 Trade-offs: stimulus risks currency depreciation and imported inflation.
 Global context matters – if trading partners are also weak, export-led recovery is limited.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                          13
 Qualifiers
 Depends on the cause of the gap (demand shock vs supply weakness).
 Depends on the state of public finances and monetary policy space.
 Depends on whether it is cyclical or signals lower potential (hysteresis).
 Likely a policy mix (fiscal + monetary + supply-side) is optimal.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                              14
 Section C.
 5. US-China trade tensions trigger a new round of tariffs on electric vehicles and
 semiconductors
 5 1 With the aid of a diagram, explain the economic impact of a trade war on the global economy.
 [10]

                                 AO1                                                AO3
Band                           6 marks                                            4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                         3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                               Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of trade war impacts. Clear   Strong chains linking tariffs to price rises,
           welfare-loss diagram with tariff/retaliation.     consumption losses, retaliation spirals and global
                                                             welfare loss.
2          3-4 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                    Limited analysis
           Diagram accurate but underdeveloped; impacts      Weak chains.
           more superficial.
1          1-2 marks                                         1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                 Limited analysis
           Basic; diagram missing/wrong.                     Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                           0 marks
           No knowledge.                                     No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1
 A trade war is a sustained cycle of tariff increases and retaliation between countries, typically
 initiated as industrial or geopolitical policy.
 Tariffs raise the domestic price of imports, reducing imports and consumer surplus, increasing
 producer surplus and government tariff revenue.
 Standard tariff diagram: D/S, world price P■, tariff raises to P■+t. Two triangles of welfare loss:
 production inefficiency + consumption inefficiency. A rectangle of tariff revenue. A transfer to
 producers.
 Retaliation: when trading partners impose reciprocal tariffs, effects are amplified.

 AO3 (impact on global economy)
 Prices rise for consumers in both economies; net real income falls.
 Global value chains are disrupted – intermediate inputs cross borders multiple times, so tariffs
 compound through production stages.
 Misallocation of resources: inefficient domestic producers replace cheaper imports – productive
 inefficiency and deadweight loss.
 Uncertainty depresses investment in exporting firms and global FDI flows.
 Export industries in both countries suffer retaliation-driven market access loss.
 Firms relocate production, incurring adjustment costs.
 Global GDP lower – IMF estimates US-China trade war cost global GDP ~0.8% by 2020.
 Disproportionate impact on developing countries integrated into global value chains.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                                  15
 Inflationary pressures – tariffs are a tax on imports, passed on to consumers.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                  16
 5 2 Discuss whether protectionism can ever be economically justified in a modern interconnected
 economy. [20]

                      AO1                              AO3                                AO4
Band                6 marks                         6 marks                            8 marks
3        5-6 marks                      5-6 marks                        6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge            Excellent analysis               Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of         Strong reasoning across infant   Reasoned judgment balancing
         arguments for and against      industry, dumping, strategic     theoretical justifications against
         protectionism.                 trade, national security         practical costs.
                                        arguments.
2        3-4 marks                      3-4 marks                        3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                 Good analysis                    Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                Good argument.                   Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                      1-2 marks                        1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge              Limited analysis                 Limited evaluation
         Few points.                    Superficial.                     Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                        0 marks                          0 marks
         No knowledge.                  No analysis.                     No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For justified protectionism)
 Infant industry argument (List): temporary protection allows nascent industries to achieve
 economies of scale and move down the learning curve (Korean/Japanese steel, automobiles).
 National security: strategic industries (defence, semiconductors, food, energy) should not be fully
 exposed to foreign supply risks (current US chip restrictions).
 Anti-dumping: tariffs justified when foreign firms sell below cost to destroy domestic competitors.
 Protection against unfair practices: subsidies, currency manipulation, IP theft can justify
 counter-measures.
 Optimal tariff argument: a large country can improve its terms of trade through tariffs (though
 invites retaliation).
 Strategic trade theory (Krugman, Brander-Spencer): in imperfectly competitive markets with
 economies of scale, government support can shift rents to domestic firms.
 Labour and environmental standards: tariffs against countries with exploitative labour or poor
 environmental rules may be justifiable.
 Protection of jobs during structural change: reduces adjustment costs, avoids hysteresis in
 affected regions.
 Green protectionism / CBAM: border carbon adjustments protect domestic climate policy from
 carbon leakage.

 AO1/AO4 (Against protectionism)
 Comparative advantage shows free trade maximises global output – protectionism is a net loss to
 the world economy.
 Tariffs are a regressive tax on consumers, hitting lower-income households hardest (essential
 imports).


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                              17
 Protected industries become uncompetitive and dependent on continued protection (capture,
 lobbying).
 Retaliation wipes out any national gains and can escalate unpredictably.
 Disrupts global supply chains, raising costs for domestic firms using imported inputs.
 Reduces dynamic efficiency: protected firms face less competitive pressure to innovate.
 Undermines international rules (WTO) and trust, with long-term systemic damage.
 Domestic producers often capture policy for private gain at public cost (public choice).
 Structural adjustment is better handled through active labour market policy and redistribution rather
 than trade barriers.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on the industry (strategic vs mature).
 Depends on whether protection is time-limited and conditional (e.g. conditional on productivity
 improvements).
 Depends on the credibility of the policy commitment to phase out protection.
 Depends on the international context – multilateral coordination preferable to unilateral.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                     18
 6. World Bank approves $50bn debt relief for Highly Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC)
 6 1 Explain the causes and consequences of unsustainable debt burdens in developing countries.
 [10]

                                AO1                                                AO3
Band                           6 marks                                           4 marks
3          5-6 marks                                          3-4 marks
           Excellent knowledge                                Good analysis
           Excellent knowledge of causes (commodity shocks,   Strong chains on consequences (debt service
           rate rises, currency depreciation, fiscal          crowding out, austerity, currency crises).
           mismanagement, IMF structural adjustment).
2          3-4 marks                                          1-2 marks
           Good knowledge                                     Limited analysis
           Narrower range.                                    Weak chains.
1          1-2 marks                                          1-2 marks
           Limited knowledge                                  Limited analysis
           Basic.                                             Unconvincing.
0          0 marks                                            0 marks
           No knowledge.                                      No analysis.


 Indicative content
 AO1 (causes)
 Historical: 1970s oil shocks and recycling of petrodollars into lending to developing countries.
 Subsequent commodity price collapse left many unable to service debt.
 Commodity price volatility: export-dependent countries see revenues collapse in downturns,
 while debt obligations remain (Prebisch-Singer).
 Exchange rate depreciation: debt typically denominated in USD; local currency depreciation
 raises real burden (original sin).
 Rising global interest rates: 1980s Volcker shock; recent Fed tightening cycle pushed up
 financing costs for emerging markets.
 Fiscal mismanagement and corruption: debt-funded spending on non-productive projects,
 capital flight to offshore accounts.
 Political instability / conflict: reduces revenue, raises security spending, destroys productive
 capacity.
 IMF structural adjustment programmes: austerity and liberalisation sometimes worsened growth
 and debt sustainability.
 Recent: Covid-19 fiscal response, war in Ukraine, climate shocks – exacerbated already high debt
 in Zambia, Ghana, Sri Lanka, Pakistan.

 AO3 (consequences)
 Debt service crowds out development spending – health, education, infrastructure starved of
 funds.
 Austerity and fiscal consolidation – painful cuts damage living standards and growth prospects.
 Currency crises: debt distress triggers capital flight, forcing devaluations that compound the debt.
 Loss of market access: defaulting countries face years of exclusion from international capital
 markets.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                            19
 Sovereign default damages banking systems, pension funds, investor confidence.
 Social unrest: imposed austerity can trigger protests (Sri Lanka 2022, Argentina).
 Environmental damage: pressure to export commodities intensifies deforestation, mining and
 over-fishing.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                          20
 6 2 Evaluate the extent to which debt relief is an effective strategy for promoting sustainable
 development. [20]

                      AO1                                 AO3                             AO4
Band                6 marks                            6 marks                       8 marks
3        5-6 marks                         5-6 marks                     6-8 marks
         Excellent knowledge               Excellent analysis            Excellent evaluation
         Excellent knowledge of HIPC,      Strong chains on how relief   Reasoned judgment considering
         MDRI, and Paris Club / G20 debt   enables investment in human   effectiveness and limits.
         frameworks.                       capital and infrastructure.
2        3-4 marks                         3-4 marks                     3-5 marks
         Good knowledge                    Good analysis                 Good evaluation
         Narrower range.                   Good argument.                Strong two-sided.
1        1-2 marks                         1-2 marks                     1-2 marks
         Limited knowledge                 Limited analysis              Limited evaluation
         Few points.                       Superficial.                  Undeveloped.
0        0 marks                           0 marks                       0 marks
         No knowledge.                     No analysis.                  No evaluation.


 Indicative content
 This answer is reversible.

 AO1/AO3 (For debt relief)
 Relieves debt overhang – countries previously spending 30–40% of revenue on interest can invest
 in education, health, infrastructure.
 HIPC Initiative and MDRI (2005 onwards) freed up billions for development spending; poverty
 reduction spending in eligible countries rose by ~2% of GDP.
 Improved human development indicators: school enrolment, immunisation coverage, child
 mortality.
 Improves creditworthiness and access to future borrowing at lower spreads.
 Reduces social tension and political instability.
 Supports the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
 Conditionality (PRSPs – Poverty Reduction Strategy Papers) channels freed resources to targeted
 development spending.
 Debt-for-nature and debt-for-climate swaps (e.g. Ecuador, Belize) simultaneously address debt
 and environmental goals.
 Sovereign bond spreads fall after debt relief, signalling improved fundamentals.

 AO1/AO4 (Against / limitations)
 Moral hazard: debt relief encourages reckless future borrowing (countries that receive relief once
 may expect it again).
 Unfairness to prudent creditors: lending countries and private bondholders take haircuts.
 Free-rider problems: non-official creditors (private banks, bondholders, increasingly China) may
 refuse to participate, reducing relief effectiveness.
 Conditionality may be ineffective or counter-productive – PRSPs can impose unsuitable
 policies on governments.


© WJEC CBAC Ltd.                                                                                         21
 Does not address underlying structural problems – primary product dependency, governance,
 institutions. Relief without reform is temporary.
 Political capture: freed-up resources may be diverted by corruption rather than flowing to poverty
 reduction.
 Recent debt crises (Zambia 2020, Sri Lanka 2022, Ghana 2022) show HIPC did not prevent
 renewed over-borrowing – countries took on new debt rapidly post-relief.
 Complex bondholder landscape (Eurobonds, Chinese bilateral loans) makes coordinated relief
 much harder than in the 1990s.
 Alternative strategies: trade preferences, FDI facilitation, climate finance, institutional reform,
 migration/remittances may produce more durable development.
 Long delays in debt restructuring (Common Framework) during which countries suffer.

 Qualifiers
 Depends on conditions attached to relief (targeted vs unconditional).
 Depends on governance capacity of recipient country.
 Depends on whether complementary reforms in creditors (bondholder coordination, G20 Common
 Framework) are effective.
 Debt relief works best as part of a wider development strategy, not alone.
 Fully credit all other relevant points.
